start.text:
The Archivists' mother-house was a conference center once, and before that a spa hotel, and before that a monastery, and the site has now completed its circuit and come home: a terraced white complex in the first fold of the control-land hills, dressed in grey robes and candlelight, built around the largest stable echo in the western country.

The Continuum's road-letter sent you here, one stop before the campus turnoff, with a warning that reads, in Voss's dot-matrix: 'The Order keeps the region's pre-Halfway records — the campus's public years included. They will have what we lost. Mind the price, and mind the Hours. They don't keep objects out there. They keep time itself, indexed. Some of it is time you may know.'

You see what she means from the approach road. The complex's southern wing — the old conference center, glass and terraces — sits inside a shine you can make out at noon: a vast, gentle, curated pocket, acres of held evening, and moving through it in slow procession, the grey robes, walking the halls of some kept summer night with censers of — you glass it, disbelieving — dust, actual dust, gathered and re-released, because the Order believes an hour is only truly kept if even its motes are congregation.

'The Archive of Hours,' Brother Alder had said at the grain church, with the hush of a man naming the capital. 'The mother-house keeps one hundred and forty-four of them. Whole hours, held stable, walked daily, prayed through. The youngest is from the morning of the Halfway. The oldest —' and he had smiled his soft smile — 'is an argument between two gardeners, in autumn, about pears. Not every scripture is dramatic.'

At the gatehouse, a tall Archivist with a ledger — there is always a ledger — takes your names, does not blink at the dog, and says the sentence that the whole Order says, gently, at every threshold, like a bell being struck: 'Everything here has already happened. You are perfectly safe. What do you need to remember?'

choices.text: Business first, and state it plainly: the Ascent campus's public-year records — personnel, contractors, the yard's construction traffic. Everything the Order holds on the decade the sky was being loaded.
Ask the gatekeeper's question back: what do the Archivists remember about the campus? The Order watched the Ascent Project build itself from these terraces — start with the witnesses, not the paper.

records_request.text:
The request goes up the Order's quiet chain and comes back down as an audience with the Prioress, who receives you in a reading room lined with card catalogues rescued, the brass plates say, from six county libraries, and who turns out to be a small, brisk, entirely un-mystical woman with a stonemason's forearms and the eyes of a chief librarian in a district of arsonists.

'Campus records. Public years.' She has them, of course; the Order bought, begged, and carried the region's paper for four years while everyone else burned it for heat. 'Payroll ledgers, contractor manifests, the freight registers for the eastern yard — yes, the Anchorage; we hold its whole civil construction file; you are the second party ever to ask for it.' She lets that land with a librarian's timing. 'The first was a letter, nineteen months ago. Typed. No return address. It asked for one page to be removed and burned.' A pause. 'We do not remove pages. We wrote back to say so, care of nowhere. The page in question is a delivery register, and you may see it with the rest — after the price.'

The price of the archive is the Order's one price, scaled to the asking: not a memory this time, but a day. 'Twenty-four hours, told truly — a whole day of your lives, each of you, deposed into the record with a keeper. We find,' she says, dry as her catalogues, 'that people who want the past tend to undervalue their own. The deposition cures that. You may choose the day.' She stands, brisk, concluding. 'Rooms in the north wing. Depositions at dusk. And —' at the door she pauses, and the librarian surfaces wholly for a moment, curiosity naked and professional — 'the register page the letter wanted burned. You'll want to know which one. Eastern yard, inbound freight, the last October before the Halfway. One line flagged for destruction, nineteen months ago, by somebody with no address.' She looks at you both with frank interest. 'Whatever you three are walking toward, it minds its paper trail. Sleep well.'

choices.text: Depose the days at dusk — pay honestly, choose your days carefully — and open the construction file in the morning.
Walk the cloister once before dusk, pricing which day of your life you can afford to spend truly — then the deposition, and in the morning, the file.

witness_request.text:
You ask for witnesses before paper, and the gatekeeper's face does the small soft thing the grey robes do instead of smiling, and by evening you are at the long refectory table being fed barley and testimony by the Order's eldest.

They watched the Ascent Project the way villagers watch a volcano: daily, expertly, without credentials. Sister Amond, ninety, kept bees on the terraces for forty years and remembers the campus's decade in freight and light — 'the night convoys east, always east, to the yard by the cutting; and the year the lights changed, when the work went below and the buildings went dark and the paychecks kept arriving. Underground years, we called them.' Brother Casse, the Order's cartographer, drew the campus from the ridge annually like a portrait sitter aging, and his folio shows it: the surface withering, gate traffic thinning, and one thing thickening — the cable runs east, trenched and re-trenched, wider every year, 'like a root system feeding one fruit.'

And there is a thing the witnesses agree on that no paper would have told you. In the last two years before the Halfway, the campus's remaining staff — the skeleton crew, the interface people — stopped coming through the village at all, except one. 'One car,' Sister Amond says, turning her cup with both hands. 'Every few weeks. Down from the campus, east through the valley, gone two days, back. Same driver, alone. Young man. Bought honey twice.' She looks at you across forty years of bees and four of ash, mild as milk. 'You drive the same way he did, dear. I watched you come up the approach. Both hands low, and leaning forward at the bends, like the car owed you an answer. I'm old, and the light was going. But I'd have said hello.'

choices.text: Sit with that in front of the household, out loud, per treaty — and at dusk, pay the Order's price for the paper: a day of your lives each, deposed truly. The construction file opens in the morning.
Thank the beekeeper properly — for the hello she would have said — and carry her sentence to the household out loud, per treaty, before the dusk deposition.

deposition.text:
The depositions take the evening, in candled cells with patient keepers, and the Order was right about the cure: nothing revalues your own hours like spending one whole day of them, told truly, into someone else's ledger.

Osy deposes a day from the year alone — 'day two hundred and six; it had a dog in it, a stray; different dog,' is all she reports afterward, flat as slate, and B leans on her all evening in a way that suggests the cell walls are thin. You depose the day the receiver locked: the legal pad, the stairs, the grandmother's go see what wants you, the voice at sixty-one seconds. Your keeper, a young man with a copyist's hands, weeps quietly and unashamedly at the tunnel dark, pen never stopping, and thanks you at the end 'for a day with a door in it.'

It is midnight, crossing the cloister under the two wrong moons of a quilted sky, that you see the southern wing up close: the Archive of Hours, its glass terraces shining their acres of kept evening into the dark, the grey procession moving through it, censers swinging. And at the cloister rail, watching it, stands the Prioress, who does not turn, and says, in the voice of a woman continuing a conversation she started privately an hour ago:

'Hour 121. The one being walked now, on the far terrace. We keep summary cards on all of them — date, place, souls, weather. Most hours we caught wild, wherever the Halfway left them stuck. But the southern wing was a conference center, and its own last evenings self-held with unusual clarity, and 121 is one of those. A works gathering, the card says. The eighth of June, eleven years and one month ago. A terrace party — an analytics firm and its client's public launch. Forty-one souls, warm night, string lights.' She turns, now, and her librarian's eyes hold yours with terrible gentleness. 'The intake index lists every soul the keepers could identify. Your deposition tonight gave us your voice, and the keepers cross-reference; it is what keepers are. You are in Hour 121, the two of you. Eleven years younger, out on that terrace, at a party neither of you has mentioned. I thought you should hear it at a rail, at night, from a librarian —' the dry surfaces, kindly — 'rather than from a card catalogue in the morning.'

choices.text: The eighth of June. You know the date the way you know your own scars. Ask her the only question that matters: can the Hour be walked by its own congregation?
Stand at the rail a while with the date in your mouth — the eighth of June, Hour 121 — and then ask her the only question that matters: can the Hour be walked by its own congregation?

hour_threshold.text:
'Walked, yes,' the Prioress says. 'Wisely — that I cannot issue from a rail.'

She takes you down at dawn, before the processions, through the silent glass corridors of the southern wing, where held evenings stand behind each doorway like water behind lock-gates — a wedding here, a quarrel there, the two gardeners and their pears — to the far terrace door, and its summary card in a brass frame: HOUR 121. 8 JUNE. TERRACE. 41 SOULS. WARM, CLEAR, STRING LIGHTS. WORKS GATHERING — LAUNCH. INDEXED SOULS: 33. And on the index, halfway down each column, in a copyist's hand: two names you know better than any other names.

'The Hour runs whole,' she says. 'Twenty-one forty to twenty-two forty. The congregation shines; you will not. They cannot see you; you will be standing in what has already happened, and nothing in it can be changed, which the Order holds to be the definition of safety and certain visitors have called by other names.' She fits the key, and does not yet turn it. 'Doctrine requires I say this, and I find I would say it anyway: we keep the Hours because kept time tells the truth. Memory is a keeper too, but a loving one — it tidies, it kindles, it repaints. The Hour does not love you. Whatever your own keeping has made of the eighth of June, in sixty minutes on that terrace you will meet the original.' She looks from you to Osy — who has said almost nothing since midnight, who stands at the door of her own eleven-years-ago with her tournament stillness on and her hand white on B's lead — and the Prioress asks the threshold question the whole Order asks, gently, like a bell:

'What do you need to remember?'

choices.text: Walk the Hour. Together, per treaty — both eyes, the original record, whatever it holds against the versions you have each kept for eleven years.
Answer the Prioress exactly — the terrace, nine to twenty-two forty, the original — because precision is the only prayer you have ever trusted. Then walk it, together, per treaty.

the_hour.text:
The terrace at 21:40 is exactly warm, exactly candled, exactly as lost as everything else, and the party is precisely as young as it will always be.

String lights. The client's banners — you read them now with four years of hindsight and a coil of ice: ASCENT PROGRAMME — CIVIL INTERFACE LAUNCH, the public face of the thing that would break the sky — and among the shining forty-one, the analytics firm's table, and at its end, arguing amiably about seating, the two of you. Eleven years younger. You had forgotten — memory the loving keeper, tidying — how young. Your younger self wears the good shirt. Her younger self has the black queen out on the table, walking it over her knuckles like a coin trick while she talks, a habit you had entirely lost until this instant and now will never lose again.

You stand in the held warmth with the present Osy beside you, B pressed close on his lead, and you watch the hour you have privately owned for eleven years unspool at its own pace, in its own order, with its terrible original timing. The drinks. The speeches, half-heard. The drift of the party toward the rail, the two young colleagues peeling off to the terrace's dark end, as they always did, as you always would — and the conversation coming, you know it is coming, the way you know weather now: your younger self going quiet, gathering himself all evening — you had forgotten the gathering took the whole evening — and at 22:19 by the string-lit clock, saying it. The thing. Eleven years of your own keeping has the next moment worn smooth as sea glass: she was kind, you were graceful, it was sad and clean and adult, and you both went back to the party, and the friendship held, which was the point, which was always the point.

The Hour does not love you.

At 22:19 your younger self says it — badly, and too fast, exactly as remembered, that much your keeping kept — and her younger self goes still, and is kind, and it is not clean, because you had cut the next four minutes: she is kind and precise and honest — 'I saw it a while ago; I'd have said so if pretending was on the table; it isn't, and I won't leave either, so you're stuck with the truth and me both' — and your younger self, twenty-nine, hurt in the specific ugly way of a man who rehearsed a different scene, says, low, with a small laugh that does not survive its own launch: 'You'll change your mind. People do.' And takes his glass, and goes back to the lights, and leaves her at the dark rail. Alone. Where she stands — the Hour keeps its own timing, and its timing is remorseless — for eleven more minutes, walking the queen over her knuckles, looking out at a valley that still had its bridges, her face doing arithmetic with no observer to be brave for. At 22:31 she puts the queen in her pocket, and assembles her expression from parts, and goes back in to the string lights, and finds your younger self, and — you had cut this too, or never saw it — touches your shoulder, and says something that makes the young fool laugh for real, and stays at your side the rest of the Hour. Repairing it. Her doing the repairing. That same night. Eleven years before you knew there was anything to repair.

choices.text: Stand in it to 22:40. All of it, the original, at its own pace — and let the version you kept for eleven years die on this terrace where it was born.

hour_divergence.text:
The Hour closes at 22:40 mid-laugh, mid-light, and holds — the last frame of the kept evening standing in the glass like a painting of itself — and then the terrace is just a terrace at dawn, and the congregation is gone, and the three of you stand in the ordinary cold with eleven years of revision lying broken on the flagstones.

Osy speaks first. She has watched her own eleven-minutes-at-the-rail from four metres' distance with her arms folded and her jaw set, and what she says, in the flattest voice in her register, is: 'For the record: I never knew you saw none of that. All these years I assumed you'd chosen not to mention it, which read as mercy, and I rated the mercy highly.' A silence. 'You genuinely remembered yourself gallant. Eleven years.' Another silence, and then, with the precision she is never crueler than when being kindest: 'Memory is a keeper with your handwriting, colleague. It edits for the same client.'

And there it is — the thing the terrace was always going to put in your hands, the small mirror of the whole broken world: a record, and an edit, and an editor who works in your hand, for your comfort, without your sign-off. You have carried a flattering version for eleven years because it was the version you could live beside. The original was always this. The question the Order asks at every threshold has turned around and is looking at you.

What do you need to remember?

choices.text: The original. Log the Hour as it ran — his cruelty, her eleven minutes, her repair — and thank her, twenty-nine again for one sentence, for the honesty you walked away from the first time. The file keeps the true version now, at any cost to its author.

What happened, happened; but the keeping is yours. Log the facts, skip the archaeology — the young fool is eleven years dead, the friendship held, and no household is served by flogging his ghost through the file. Close the Hour and get to the freight register.

honest_memory.text:
You take out the log, on the cold terrace, before breakfast and before courage can be rationed, and you write the Hour as it ran: 22:19, said badly and too fast; 22:20, her honesty, complete; 22:23, his answer — you write the sentence out whole, 'you'll change your mind, people do,' because paraphrase is just editing with better manners — 22:23, he leaves her at the rail; 22:31, she repairs what he broke, same night, unwitnessed, unthanked. And beneath it, in the space the protocol reserves for findings: 'The flattering version is deceased. It was eleven years old. It was load-bearing. Let it fall.'

Then you close the log and you turn to her on the dawn terrace and you do the thing the young fool owed and never paid.

'Thank you,' you say. 'Eleven years late, for the honesty. You put the truth on the table at cost, with no pretending on offer and no exit taken, and I answered it with a cheap line and a turned back, and then you came in off that rail and repaired my evening with your own hands. I saw it this time. It's in the file. And for the record —' and your voice does the thing hers does, levels, because some cargo needs the flat — 'you never changed your mind, and you never owed it, and the eleven years happened anyway, and they were the best ones I had. The truth and you both. Still stuck with them. Gladly.'

Osy looks at you for a long moment in the grey light, and something moves behind the tournament stillness — cross-referenced, filed, held — and what she says, finally, is: 'Noted. Large font.' And then, turning to the terrace door, briskly, because the household does not flinch at pages but is permitted to change them: 'Breakfast, colleague. The freight register opens at eight, somebody with no address wants a line of it burned, and I intend to read that line twice.' But at the door itself she stops, one hand on the frame, not turning around, and adds, in the voice she uses for exactly one person: 'The repair took, by the way. That evening. He laughed at the right things the whole last hour, and I went home thinking — that held. It'll hold.' A beat. 'It held.'

item.name: The Original Kept
item.description: The Hour logged as it ran, the flattering version let fall, the thanks paid eleven years late.

choices.text: Breakfast, and the freight register, and the one line somebody wanted burned.
Carry it held into breakfast like contraband joy — and then the freight register, and the one line somebody wanted burned.

flattering_close.text:
You log the facts — date, place, Hour walked, record diverges from recollection, discrepancy noted — in the driest grammar the protocol allows, and you close the log on the terrace with the dawn coming up grey behind the glass.

'The young fool is dead,' you say, to her, at the rail where she once stood for eleven unwitnessed minutes. 'I'm not going to exhume him for the file. What matters held: the friendship, the eleven years, the household. The rest is archaeology.'

Osy looks at you for a moment with an expression you cannot fully price — something weighed, something shelved — and says, 'Your side of the book, your entries. That was always the deal.' And then, evenly, without heat, the way she lays down a piece she has decided not to play: 'One observation, though, colleague, from the party concerned, and then I'll drop it. You've just watched an editor in your own hand improve a record for its author's comfort, and you've ruled the original out of scope.' She shoulders her pack, and whistles low for B, and at the terrace door she delivers the rest over her shoulder, flat and final as a stamp: 'When we find the one who does that to the world, I want to know what you'll say to him. Breakfast. The register opens at eight.'

The walk to the reading room is four minutes. It has never once occurred to you before this morning that a corridor could be constructed entirely of a sentence.

choices.text: The freight register, then, and the one line somebody with no address wanted burned.
Walk the four-minute corridor without defending yourself to it — the sentence is hers to hold and yours to earn against — and open the register at eight.

freight_register.text:
The construction file of the Anchorage fills a reading-room table: four years of civil paper, freight registers, pour schedules, the yard rising in triplicate — and the household reads it the way it reads everything now, two skulls, one method, the dog asleep across the door.

The paper tells its story in the world's most honest genre, the invoice. The yard was built twice. Once, publicly, as transit maintenance — sheds, gravel, fencing, dull as designed. Then, in the last eighteen months, the second building: night freight, unlisted contractors paid in the loom-stamped scrip, and concrete — concrete beyond any accounting of sheds, poured down a shaft the paper is careful never to name except by its works code, and the works code, Osy finds within the hour, cross-referenced in a cable-plan margin in a tired engineer's economical hand: MOORING. The root system Brother Casse drew from the ridge fed one fruit, and the fruit was planted deep.

And then the line. Inbound freight, the last October, the page a letter with no address wanted burned. Among the girders and the cable drums, one entry, unremarkable to any eye that had not spent a month learning what the household has learned:

'OCT 14 — 1 (ONE) CRATE, SPECIAL HANDLING, CLIENT ESCORT — CONTENTS: CALIBRATION STANDARD (BIOLOGICAL) — CARRIED ON: MANIFEST W/HELD — ESCORT SIGNATURE: —'

And the escort's signature, faded, hurried, impatient — the crossbar not finishing the t — is yours.

Osy does not gasp; the household is past gasping. She lays her finger under the words CALIBRATION STANDARD (BIOLOGICAL), and then under the signature, and then she sits back, and looks at the reading room's high safe windows, and says, in the voice of a woman finally seeing the whole board: 'Colleague. You didn't visit the yard before the Halfway. You delivered to it. Something alive, in a crate, with your name on the escort line, one October — and nineteen months ago, from inside whatever's left, somebody who minds his paper trail tried to burn the only page that proves it.' She turns to you, and her eyes are bright and appalled and steady. 'The question was always what you are. The paper just answered what you were. You were the courier.'

item.name: The October Line
item.description: One crate, special handling, contents: CALIBRATION STANDARD (BIOLOGICAL), escort signature yours — the impatient t, unmistakable. The page somebody with no address wanted burned. You were the courier, one October, to the yard that holds the day still.

choices.text: Copy everything, thank the Order, and take the road's last leg — the campus itself, where the interface archives wait, and where the word CALIBRATION was somebody's job to define.
Sit one minute with the word courier where she can watch you take the weight of it — then copy everything, thank the Order, and take the last leg to the campus.

end_ch8.text:
The Order sees you off at the gatehouse with road bread and its unbearable gentleness, and the Prioress herself walks you to the white marker where the campus road forks from the valley.

'Librarians' farewell,' she says. 'Three facts, no charge. One: the letter that wanted the page burned was postmarked nowhere, but it was franked — machine-franked, the way institutional mailrooms do — and the frank was a transit authority's. Your yard has a mailroom, or had. Two: Hour 121 will be walked and kept whether you ever return or not; the Order does not lend the past, but it does not repossess it either; what you met on that terrace is yours now, in whatever keeping you chose.' Her eyes rest on you a half-second longer than the sentence needs. 'Three.' She looks down the fork, to the campus road climbing into the grey hills, wire and dead lights, and her briskness gentles by one degree. 'Every soul who has taken that road since the Halfway has been one of two kinds: Continuum, going home to grieve — or yours, whatever yours is. The Order has watched this fork for four years. Nothing has ever come back down it changed for the better except the bees.' She steps back. 'We'll keep your depositions safe. Go and find out what you were, children. And then — this is the librarian's whole doctrine, and it is the only one I hold — come back and file it, whatever it is. Unfiled truth just becomes somebody's edit.'

The campus road takes you up into the folded hills through the morning, three heartbeats and their shared ledger of hours, the October line riding in the pack beside the coordinates that say not yet. B walks point with his ears at cruising trim. Osy walks the file out loud, ordering it, the way she orders everything: a courier with no memory of the delivery; a crate marked biological; a yard that holds the day still around whatever was moored in it; an Editor who spends a city on her and signs in his hand.

'One hypothesis survives everything,' she says, at the crest, where the campus's dead towers first show over the moor grass. 'I've been circling it since the vault and I'll say it at the gate or I'll never say it: whatever was in that crate, colleague — whatever got moored in that yard and calibrated against a living standard — I don't think you forgot the delivery.' She stops, and looks at you, wind off the moor, the dead campus waiting. 'I think the delivery is the last thing you did remember. I think everything after it was taken the way my afternoons are taken. And I think the man who signs in your hand has been living in the difference ever since. Come on. Let's go read your personnel file.'
